retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Color Perception -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal color vision typically subdivides into congenital and acquired forms. The genetic disorders of color vision occur due to defects in genes encoding cone opsins. These genes encode the expression of cone opsins and genes for proteins involved in phototransduction (such as phosphodiesterase and cyclic nucleotide-gated channel subunits). Such defects have a wide range of possible outcomes and may feature different progression rates, retinal degeneration, and loss of visual acuity. Guanylate cyclase deficiencies can lead to Leber congenital amaurosis and severe vision loss early in life. In contrast, rod dystrophies leading to retinitis pigmentosa may cause late color vision abnormalities as degenerating rods affect cone structure and function. Additional causes of color vision defects include fundus albipunctatus and Stargardt disease. Acquired color vision deficiency, or dyschromatopsia, can occur due to any injury or disruption to visual pathways. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, G Protein Coupled Receptors -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: G-protein coupled receptors and G-proteins are prevalent throughout the human body and are involved in a wide range of physiological functions. Mutations in these proteins can lead to various diseases, including retinitis pigmentosa and cholera. Retinitis pigmentosa, caused by a mutation in a GPCR, is an eye disease in which the retina is damaged. People with this disease have blurred vision and/or difficulty seeing in low-light conditions. This eye disease is an inherited disorder, and there is no treatment. Wearing sunglasses can protect the vision that remains. There are over 800 different GPCRs in the human body. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Vision -- Clinical Significance -- 1. Congenital Stationary Night Blindness (CSNB)
retrieved_docs.content: One such disease is congenital stationary night blindness. It is a genetic defect resulting in functional cones but dysfunctional rods. In this disease, many potential culprits have been identified including abnormal rhodopsin, arrestin, rod transducin, rod phosphodiesterase, and rhodopsin kinase. Studies have demonstrated that in some populations of this disease, rods are permanently transmitting light signals. In CSNB, b-waves are reduced (in CSNB type 2) or absent (in CSNB type 1) during an electroretinogram (ERG). There are currently no treatments for this disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, G Protein Coupled Receptors -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Loss of function of GPCRs due to amino acid substitutions, truncations by nonsense or frameshift mutations, insertions, deletions, and rearrangements are also associated with many human diseases. For example, mutation of vasopressin V2 receptor (antidiuretic hormone receptor, AVPR2) and aquaporin 2 (AQP2) genes are linked to nephrogenic diabetes insipidus syndrome. Mutations in the Inactivating mutations in the rhodopsin (RHO) gene are the most common cause of retinitis pigmentosa (RP), which causes retinal dystrophy with early onset of night blindness and subsequent loss of the visual field. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Color Vision -- Clinical Significance -- Stationary Night Blindness (CSNB)
retrieved_docs.content: One such disease is congenital stationary night blindness. It is a genetic defect resulting in functional cones but dysfunctional rods. Many potential culprits have been identified for this disease, including abnormal rhodopsin, arrestin, rod transducin, rod phosphodiesterase, and rhodopsin kinase. Studies have demonstrated that in some populations of this disease, rods are stuck permanently, outputting light signals. There are currently no treatments for this disorder. In CSNB, b-waves are reduced (in CSNB type 2) or absent (in CSNB type 1) during an electroretinogram.

retrieved_docs.title: Retinitis Pigmentosa -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic mutations responsible for RP produce biochemical dysfunction that specifically affects rod photoreceptors in the retina. [8] Defects may be associated with multiple injury pathways, including apoptosis, light damage, ciliary transport dysfunction, and endoplasmic reticulum stress. The typical result of all the possible pathways is the death of the rod photoreceptors. [9] Since the rods are responsible for low-light vision, the ever-increasing loss of these cells produces the characteristic night blindness and a gradual diminution of peripheral vision. Eventually, the destruction of large numbers of rods has a deleterious effect on the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and affects cone photoreceptors. As cones succumb to the toxic environment created by progressive cell death in the retina, dyschromatopsia, or disturbance of color perception, may develop (rod-cone dystrophy).

retrieved_docs.title: Color Vision -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The light response is a one-to-one effect. The enzyme rhodopsin kinase quickly binds metarhodopsin II, phosphorylating and halting its activity. The protein arrestin binds phosphorylated metarhodopsin II. Innate GTPase activity in transducin eventually degrades bound GTP to GDP, leading to PDE dissociation and inactivation. MetarhodopsinII is unstable and splits within minutes, leading to opsin and free trans-retinal. Trans-retinal is transported to pigment epithelial cells that convert trans-retinal back to 11-cis-retinal, which eventually is recombined with opsin within cones/rods to reform rhodopsin. Guanylate cyclase restores cGMP concentration, and the cone/receptor is ready to respond to another light exposure event.

retrieved_docs.title: Retinal Dystrophies -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Retinal dystrophies, or inherited retinal diseases (IRD), represent diverse genetic conditions affecting the retina, including rod-cone dystrophies, cone-rod dystrophies, chorioretinal degenerations, and macular dystrophies. These disorders are characterized by a gradual loss of vision, ranging from night blindness and color vision deficiencies to complete blindness in advanced cases. More than 270 genes have been linked to various retinal dystrophy types, with clinical presentations varying even among family members with the same mutation.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Night Vision -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Rhodopsin is the photopigment in rods. It is a G-protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) consisting of the protein scotopsin and the Vitamin A–derived cofactor retinol. Light exposure allows retinol's isomerization from its 11-cis-retinal configuration into the active all-trans-retinal conformation. Isomerization of retinol into its active all-trans-retinal conformation then sets off a cascade of changes, resulting in transformation into metarhodopsin II (Meta II). Meta II activates the transducin protein, followed by the transducin alpha subunit activating cyclic guanosine monophosphate phosphodiesterase (cGMP phosphodiesterase). In the resting or dark state, cGMP directly activates cation channels that cause net depolarization of rod photoreceptors (approximately -40 mV), continuously releasing glutamate neurotransmitter that hyperpolarizes some surrounding cells and depolarizes others. In the activation or light pathway, transducin alpha subunit activation of cGMP phosphodiesterase breaks down cGMP into GMP, lowering cellular levels of cGMP and thus decreasing cation channel activity. The decreased cation channel activity causes the hyperpolarization of the rod photoreceptor and reduces the release of excitatory glutamate neurotransmitters by the rod cell. This increase in photon absorption and decreased glutamate release is a light sensation. Rod cells exhibit significant signal amplification, as each rhodopsin GPCR may activate as many as 800 transducin proteins. Reversal of rods to the resting state is mediated by arrestin, rhodopsin kinase (RK), and the closure of cGMP channels. RK phosphorylates the cytosolic tail of rhodopsin, decreasing transducin activity. Arrestin increases GTP to GDP hydrolysis, thereby inactivating transducin, a G protein. The decreased intracellular calcium, caused by the closure of cGMP-sensitive cation channels in the activating pathways, triggers intracellular proteins to activate guanylate cyclase, which restores cGMP levels. These pathways allow for plasma membrane depolarization in the restored resting state of rod cells. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Color Vision -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Within rhodopsin, light absorption leads to a chemical reaction that forces part of the rhodopsin molecule to translocate by changing protein conformation and exposing active sites. This activated form of rhodopsin is known as metarhodopsin. MetarhodopsinII activates many copies of the G protein transducin (by replacing transducin's GDP with GTP). Activated transducin complexes and activated cyclic nucleotide phosphodiesterase (PDE) can hydrolyze 1000 molecules of cGMP to 5'-GMP per second. cGMP-gated channels in the plasma membrane of these rods (or cones) allow sodium ion influx at high cGMP concentrations; this is balanced by cation exchanger-mediated glutamate efflux, maintaining cell depolarization (dark conditions). At low cGMP concentrations, these channels close, stopping sodium ion influx and reducing glutamate efflux, leading to cell hyperpolarization (light conditions). Thus, light-induced rod/cone state changes lead to hyperpolarization of the photoreceptor cells, which cease to transmit the neurotransmitter glutamate. Conversely, photoreceptor cells without the presence of light exist in the depolarized state and continuously release glutamate.

retrieved_docs.title: Full-Field Electroretinogram -- Clinical Significance -- Vitamin A Deficiency
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin A is an important component of rhodopsin, a photosensitive pigment important for rod function. Patients with deficient levels of vitamin A, whether from malnutrition, malabsorption, or impaired liver metabolism, experience night blindness secondary to rod dysfunction, and the ffERG shows a depressed dark-adapted response.

retrieved_docs.title: Retinal Dystrophies -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Phototransduction : The mutations affecting phototransduction may involve multiple genes, including rhodopsin ( RHO ), PDE6 , and cyclic nucleotide-gated (CNG) channels. [23] Disc morphogenesis : A crucial gene for disc morphogenesis includes PRPH2 . [24] Ciliary formation or trafficking : Major genes controlling cilia formation include USH2A (affected in Usher syndrome), RPGR , CEP290 , and BBS1-22 (affected in Bardet-Biedl syndrome). The syndromes associated with ciliopathy and retinal dystrophies include Usher, Bardet-Biedl, Joubert, Senior–Løken, and Alstrom syndromes. [25] [26] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Retinal Dystrophies -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Rods and cone photoreceptors are the central cellular units responsible for visual phototransduction. [5] Phototransduction is a process by which light signals are converted into action potentials within the retina, facilitating the brain's image perception. During this process, light-sensitive pigments are generated and recycled. Etiologies of retinal dystrophies include abnormalities in photoreceptors and defects in phototransduction.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Night Vision -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin A, a fat-soluble vitamin primarily obtained from the diet in beta-carotene-containing foods, deficiency can also cause nyctalopia. Vitamin A is a precursor substrate to 11-cis-retinal in the retinal rod photoreceptors. The rhodopsin system is sensitive to dietary Vitamin A deficiency and decreased Vitamin A intake may lead to low intracellular levels of 11-cis-retinal in the resting state, impairing dark adaption and manifesting as symptoms of night blindness. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Photopic Vision -- Clinical Significance -- Disorders of Photopic Vision
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of photopic vision typically result from mutations in one of many proteins critical for cone or retinal pigment epithelium function. [15] The most common cone disorder is color blindness, a relatively benign disorder in which a single opsin gene mutation (usually M- or L-opsin, both present on the X chromosome). [15] More severe inherited cone disorders include achromatopsia, in which most or all cones are nonfunctional, cone-only dystrophy, mixed rod-cone dystrophies, and cone dysfunctions with a syndromic cause. Signs of cone dysfunction include color vision abnormalities, reduced visual acuity, photophobia, and nystagmus. [16] The therapeutic approach for patients with these disorders is an interprofessional and depends heavily on the degree of disability. Individuals with simple color blindness may need no assistance, while patients with retinal degeneration may require extensive medical and occupational therapy. [17] Developing visual restoration approaches include gene therapy, regenerative medicine, and visual prosthetics (varying evidence levels from I to V). [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Retinal Dystrophies -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mutations in various genes cause retinal dystrophies. These mutations can be inherited in an autosomal dominant, autosomal recessive, X-linked, or mitochondrial manner. The specific gene mutations determine the type of IRD, such as retinitis pigmentosa, Leber congenital amaurosis, or Stargardt disease. The primary pathology in retinal dystrophies involves the dysfunction and death of photoreceptor cells (rods and cones) and the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE), including:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2049
retrieved_docs.content: The term “vrozené” (congenital) is an allowed qualifier for both “فرط البروتينات الشحمية” and “انفصال الشبكية,” indicating that these conditions can be described as congenital forms. Recognizing the congenital nature of hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) and retinal detachment (انفصال الشبكية) is important for accurate diagnosis, genetic counseling, and tailored patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
retrieved_docs.content: and choroid. This hierarchical relationship clarifies the scope of ablative retinal therapies. Additionally, the disease referred to in Arabic as “داء ريفسام” manifests its clinical findings in the retina, reinforcing that retinal involvement—whether from systemic metabolic disorders, primary retinal diseases, or targeted retinal lesion destruction—requires precise identification of the retinal site for accurate diagnosis and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2169
retrieved_docs.content: Acute disorder of hemodialysis, sudden acquired retinal degeneration, and متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى (dialysis dysfunction syndrome) are all described as having a clinical course characterized by sudden onset. The “sudden onset” qualifier indicates that the manifestations of these conditions develop rapidly, which is a key feature for clinicians to recognize when diagnosing and managing these disorders. Understanding that a rapid, abrupt presentation is common to these entities helps guide timely evaluation and appropriate therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1709
retrieved_docs.content: The condition تمزق الشبكية (retinal tear) is directly linked to the need for repair of retinal tear; detachment, indicating that a retinal tear often requires surgical repair to prevent or treat detachment. This relationship highlights the importance of timely intervention for retinal tears to preserve visual function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 266
retrieved_docs.content: and retina, leading to scarring, atrophy, detachment, neovascularization and visual impairment. Accurate identification of the specific choroidal and retinal pathology is essential for comprehensive evaluation, multidisciplinary management and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus—all localized to the retina. The retina is also the primary site for retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and various other retinopathies. Therapeutic interventions targeting these lesions comprise destructive procedures on the posterior segment of the eye, categorized under “destruction of lesion of retina and choroid,” with radiation‑based destruction of localized retinal lesions (e.g., macular edema or tumors) as a further sub‑category. The disease داء ريفسام is likewise identified with retinal findings, underscoring the eye’s role in its manifestation. Clinical assessment employs the concept of “general clinical state” to interpret both biologic function failure and uncontrolled disease conditions; uncontrolled disease conditions are associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, highlighting the need for vigilant monitoring. Biologic function failure is linked to hepatic‑renal failure, indicating a critical organ‑system

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1079
retrieved_docs.content: (isolated choroidal inflammation), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية (combined choroidal‑retinal inflammation), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد (focal unspecified combined inflammation), and حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي (hereditary choroidal atrophy). Collectively, these conditions demonstrate the complex interplay of vascular occlusion, hemorrhage, inflammation, metabolic injury, and degenerative remodeling within the retina and choroid; recognizing each as a child of “Disorders of choroid and retina (H30‑H36)” aids clinicians in systematic diagnosis, risk stratification, and the selection of targeted therapeutic strategies to preserve visual function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is linked by genetika to a range of distinct medical conditions, including تفاعل نقل الدم (blood transfusion reaction), gout and other crystal arthropathies, انفصال الشبكية (retinal detachment), and فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ (hyperlipidemia). This genetic association suggests that inherited factors may influence susceptibility to transfusion reactions, crystal‑induced joint disease, retinal structural failure, and disorders of lipid metabolism. Recognizing these genetic connections can enhance risk assessment, guide personalized therapeutic approaches, and support preventive strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 47
retrieved_docs.content: levels (نقص كالسيوم الدم), highlighting a metabolic link that can influence patient management. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that bone‑marrow diagnostic procedures are central to identifying specific anemia subtypes, that such subtypes fit within the larger framework of deficiency and immune‑mediated disorders, and that the resulting diagnostic and metabolic insights guide both therapeutic decisions and the understanding of associated complications such as retinal vascular disease and calcium dysregulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: radiation implantation to destroy localized retinal lesions such as macular edema or tumors—are a child procedure of the broader “destructive posterior‑segment” category and are performed directly on the retina and choroid. The retina also serves as the finding site for the disease “داء ريفسام”. Together, these relationships demonstrate how functional failure, chronic disease trajectories, and interpretive impairments intersect across cardiovascular, renal, metabolic, gastrointestinal, and neurological systems, and they highlight the multidisciplinary procedural approaches required for managing such complex clinical scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ) is clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). Both conditions represent severe microvascular complications of long‑standing diabetes mellitus, and the presence of diabetic retinopathy often signals an increased risk of progression to advanced kidney failure. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians, as early detection and management of retinal changes can prompt more aggressive monitoring of renal function, and coordinated care can help mitigate the morbidity associated with simultaneous vision loss and the need for renal replacement therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 192
retrieved_docs.content: of retina and choroid” is the broad category; its child “destructive procedure on posterior segment of eye” includes the specific procedure “destruction of localized lesion of retina (e.g., macular edema, tumors) by radiation source implantation,” all of which act directly on the retina. Collectively, this network integrates cardiologic failure of the mitral valve, diverse insertion‑based interventions, chronic gastrointestinal and diabetic disease courses, genetic cystic fibrosis phenotypes, neurocognitive impairment in Feigenbaum‑Bergeron‑Richardson syndrome, and the spectrum of secondary diabetes mellitus complications—including retinal and renal pathology—underscoring the need for coordinated assessment and management across cardiology, ophthalmology, nephrology, gastroenterology, genetics, and neurology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus, its specific complications (various forms of retinopathy and nephropathy), and the relationship between these complications and ocular destructive procedures is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: داء ريفسام, indicating that ocular examination of the retina can reveal manifestations of this disease and underscoring the importance of retinal assessment for accurate diagnosis and management of patients suspected of having داء ريفسام. Understanding the relationships among these insertion procedures, retinal‑targeted radiation therapies, and the ocular and renal sequelae of secondary diabetes mellitus enables clinicians to select appropriate interventional techniques, monitor disease‑specific complications, and provide integrated care that addresses both procedural interventions and systemic effects, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2716
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 is identified as مرض السكري. مرض السكري is clinically associated with pancreatic disorders (not diabetes) and with اضطراب في الشبكية, indicating that patients with diabetes may also present with other pancreatic abnormalities and retinal disturbances. In addition, مرض السكري has rizikové faktory, highlighting the presence of specific risk factors that contribute to the development and progression of the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2602
retrieved_docs.content: Acute disorder of hemodialysis, First use syndrome of dialysis, Sudden acquired retinal degeneration, and متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى (dialysis dysfunction syndrome) are medical conditions that share a common clinical characteristic: a sudden‑onset course. In each of these disorders the disease manifests abruptly, indicating that their clinical course is defined by a rapid appearance of symptoms. Recognizing this sudden‑onset pattern is important for timely diagnosis and appropriate management of complications related to hemodialysis, initial dialysis use, retinal degeneration, or dialysis dysfunction.
